stories.body: Andy Burnham confirmed during a train ride from Manchester to London that he will run for Labour leader and thus prime minister, and within hours likely rival Wes Streeting abandoned his own ambitions to endorse him, prompting speculation that the race is effectively over despite calls from some MPs for a contest to test Burnham’s still-unclear national platform; Angela Rayner urged the party to refocus on governing, Darren Jones is being pressed by Starmer loyalists to stand though allies doubt he will, and questions persist over Burnham’s grasp of fiscal rules after a much-criticized Newsnight interview.
stories.headline: UK Labour MPs mull contests even as Burnham’s bid clears the field

stories.body: Following Swiss-hosted talks that mediators say produced a 60-day roadmap toward a broader accord, the United States issued an emergency waiver allowing Iranian crude and petrochemical sales in dollars—including direct imports into the US—while Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Tehran agreed to keep the Strait of Hormuz open and readmit IAEA inspectors, claims Iranian officials immediately rejected even as Vice President JD Vance insisted discussions with inspectors could begin within days and linked the relief to reopening Hormuz and de-confliction steps for a regional cease-fire.
stories.headline: US sanctions waiver lets Iran sell oil for dollars amid disputed nuclear inspection pledges

stories.body: US Central Command said American forces shot down four Iranian drones aimed at maritime traffic in the Strait of Hormuz and then struck surveillance radar sites on Qeshm Island and near Goruk, an escalation that comes as Tehran demands sanctions relief, access to frozen oil revenue and leverage over Hormuz before agreeing to an interim deal to halt the four-month-old war, while Iran simultaneously conditions any broader accord with Washington on a cease-fire between Israel and Hezbollah and the withdrawal of Israeli troops from southern Lebanon.
stories.headline: US hits Iranian coastal radar after drones target Hormuz shipping

stories.body: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi reaffirmed that any peace deal with Washington must coincide with an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon and a cease-fire covering Hezbollah, warning that Lebanon will be integral to future agreements as clashes continue despite US-mediated pauses, and in a separate sign of geopolitical strain European officials drew fierce criticism from rights advocates and MEPs for inviting a Taliban delegation to Brussels to discuss deportations, a move opponents say legitimizes a regime accused of erasing women from public life.
stories.headline: Iran ties truce talks to Lebanon while EU plan to host Taliban sparks outrage

stories.body: Kyiv’s mayor said a nine-story apartment block and multiple vehicles burned after debris from a heavy Russian barrage hit the capital’s Podil and Obolon districts, sending smoke plumes skyward as President Volodymyr Zelenskiy warned residents to heed air-raid alerts because intelligence indicates Moscow is preparing another massive strike on command centers and military-linked targets.
stories.headline: Kyiv braces for new large-scale strikes as Russian missiles ignite city fires

stories.body: Ousmane Sonko, freshly reinstated as parliamentary speaker after President Bassirou Diomaye Faye fired him as prime minister, said Pastef will refuse seats in the next government because of unresolved disagreements over the party’s executive role, deepening political turmoil just as Dakar seeks to restart a $1.8 billion IMF program frozen over misreported debt that pushed end-2024 liabilities to 132% of GDP.
stories.headline: Senegal’s Pastef rejects cabinet posts amid IMF stand-off

stories.body: The US Senate passed a $70 billion supplemental for Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol on a 52-47 vote, rebuffing Democratic efforts to eliminate Donald Trump’s $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization” fund despite assurances from acting Attorney General Todd Blanche that the Justice Department would shelve it, leaving House consideration for next week as Republicans argue the money is vital for deportation crackdowns and Democrats warn it could become a slush fund to reimburse political allies.
stories.headline: Senate approves $70 billion ICE boost while keeping Trump’s contested fund intact

stories.body: A Public First survey for Politico found 44% of US adults believe wagering on election outcomes should be illegal and similar shares oppose markets on presidential statements or pardons, even as platforms such as Kalshi and Polymarket see hundreds of millions of dollars traded on the 2028 race following a court ruling that expanded regulated political betting; critics like Sen. Jeff Merkley say the industry risks introducing corruption into democracy, while proponents argue the markets provide real-time forecasting data.
stories.headline: Poll shows Americans uneasy with prediction-market betting on politics

stories.body: Campaign finance records reviewed by Politico indicate Arizona Sen. Ruben Gallego has for years used his committees to cover family trips to Miami, Chicago, Disneyland and Disney World, reimbursed more than $18,000 in child-care expenses—including payments to his mother-in-law—and co-hosted a $5,000-per-ticket Super Bowl fundraiser with Eric Swalwell, spending patterns critics say resemble a personal slush fund even though leadership PAC rules allow broad discretion; Gallego defends the spending as compliant and routine for politicians with young families.
stories.headline: Disclosures show Sen. Ruben Gallego used donor funds for family travel and child care

stories.body: The National Independent Venue Association and Fan Alliance asked congressional leaders to ban speculative ticket listings, cap resale markups and force secondary platforms to report fulfillment and complaint data after dozens of fans said they were left without valid World Cup seats despite spending thousands of dollars through sites like StubHub, which blames FIFA’s ticket-transfer technology while the tournament organizer insists only its official portal guarantees delivery.
stories.headline: World Cup ticket scams spur venue groups to demand resale crackdown

stories.body: Spain’s Supreme Court unanimously sentenced former transport minister and PSOE organization chief José Luis Ábalos to 24 years and three months in prison—of which he must serve up to 16 and a half—for crimes including criminal organization, bribery, embezzlement and influence peddling tied to overpriced COVID-era mask contracts, while ex-adviser Koldo García received 19 years and eight months and businessman Víctor de Aldama got four and a half years but avoided incarceration thanks to extensive cooperation that also spared him repayment of €3.7 million in commissions.
stories.headline: Supreme Court jails ex-minister José Luis Ábalos for pandemic mask graft

stories.body: Spanish ministers privately interpret the Supreme Court’s leniency toward cooperating businessman Víctor de Aldama as a signal to other witnesses to testify against the Socialist Party in related corruption probes, after Aldama left court praising the sentence and saying he hoped others would now collaborate, a message some in the executive view as an invitation to fabricate accusations to damage the government.
stories.headline: Government sees ruling as attempt to coax witnesses against PSOE

stories.body: The General Council of the Judiciary voted, using chair Isabel Perelló’s tie-breaking ballot, to let disciplinary chief Ricardo Conde decide whether to open a case against Judge Juan Carlos Peinado after he suggested in a ruling that the police detail protecting Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez’s wife, Begoña Gómez, might help her flee justice; Peinado has meanwhile ordered Gómez to appear Wednesday to surrender her passport along with Moncloa adviser Cristina Álvarez.
stories.headline: Judicial watchdog moves to discipline judge over comments on PM’s wife

stories.body: The city will keep the pandemic-era reversal of traffic directions on Travessera de Gràcia after mobility data showed daily vehicle counts plunged up to 87% on central stretches, freeing space for pedestrians and satisfying local shopkeepers even as some residents complain of harder car access and congestion spillover onto Torrent de l’Olla; officials plan a new traffic light at Milà i Fontanals and route changes for bus 114 to improve eastbound connections.
stories.headline: Barcelona makes Travessera de Gràcia’s traffic-calming permanent after steep vehicle drop

stories.body: Barcelona transported the 300-ton bearing for the tunnel-boring machine that will drive the Ferrocarrils L8 extension from Plaça Espanya to Gràcia, hauling the six-meter-wide piece overnight from Sant Boi with police escorts and precision maneuvers at Gran Via’s launch shaft, and will continue delivering the remaining eight components, including the screw conveyor, through mid-July ahead of tunneling slated to start this summer.
stories.headline: 200-ton tuneladora component arrives for FGC L8 extension

stories.body: Barcelona’s federation of neighborhood associations (FAVB) and local groups say tourism pressure is now year-round in areas such as Sagrada Família, Park Güell and the beaches, urging City Hall to permanently increase metro and bus frequencies in saturated zones, redirect at least half of tourist tax revenues from promotion to mitigation, compel major attractions to fund impact measures, and create standing dialogue tables so residents can co-design policies that reduce overcrowding and protect housing and public space.
stories.headline: Neighborhood groups demand permanent transit boosts and tourist-impact compensation

stories.body: Barcelona finished a €22.8 million program to boost groundwater distribution capacity by 20%—the equivalent of 60 Olympic pools—with three new reservoirs on Montjuïc enabling two-thirds of the hill’s water use to come from non-potable aquifer supplies for irrigation, street cleaning, firefighting and fountains; Mayor Jaume Collboni said the investment, which also adds 21 hectares of green areas irrigated with subterranean water across the city, is crucial after the 2023 drought killed about 9,000 trees even though regional reservoirs are now full.
stories.headline: City completes 20% expansion of groundwater network to irrigate parks
